citation: Acemoglu & Restrepo (2019), Automation and New Tasks: How Technology Displaces and Reinstates Labor
research_question: How does automation displace labor and how can new tasks reinstate labor demand?
setting_sample: Theoretical framework; US labor market context
treatment_or_exposure: Task-based automation vs. new task creation
main_findings: Automation reduces labor share; new tasks can restore it. Long-run growth depends on the race between automation and new task creation.
limitations: Theoretical model; limited direct empirical testing at time of publication
relevance_to_project: Core theoretical anchor: task-based model of automation displacement vs. new-task reinstatement. Foundation for our channel decomposition.
open_questions: How to operationalize automation vs new-task channels in cross-country panel with compute as treatment?

citation: Acemoglu & Restrepo (2018), The Race between Man and Machine: Implications of Technology for Growth, Factor Shares, and Employment
research_question: What are the implications of automation technology for economic growth, factor shares, and employment?
identification_strategy: Task-based model with endogenous technology choice
main_findings: Technology can reduce labor share and employment unless new tasks are created sufficiently fast. The race between automation and new tasks determines growth outcomes.
limitations: Calibrated model; not direct cross-country empirical test
relevance_to_project: Theoretical foundation for understanding how compute capacity (as a proxy for automation technology) affects growth through labor share and employment channels.
open_questions: Does the race framework apply to developing countries with different industrial structures?

citation: Acemoglu (2025), The Simple Macroeconomics of AI
research_question: What are the aggregate macroeconomic effects of AI on productivity and growth?
setting_sample: Macro-level estimates based on task exposure and AI substitution elasticities
data_sources: Occupational task data, AI exposure estimates

A central finding is that the automation and robotics literature is comparatively developed, while credible causal macro evidence on AI itself remains sparse and fragmented. citation: Fruits & Stout (2026), AI, Productivity, and Labor Markets: A Review of the Empirical Evidence
doi: no_doi: working paper or not indexed
research_question: Literature review: AI → productivity and labor market effects
main_findings: This issue brief reviews the empirical literature regarding the economic impact of generative AI through 2025. The authors discuss the “jagged frontier” of performance: while AI significantly boosts productivity in specific tasks like coding, writing, and customer service—often by 15% to 50%—these gains are most pronounced for lower-skilled workers, leading to “skill compression.” Despite fears of mass unemployment, aggregate data show limited labor-market disruption. Instead of widespread job loss, the evidence suggests a shift in “entry-level” roles, where AI automates tasks typically assigned to junior staff. Macroeconomic growth remains difficult to measure due to the “productivity J-curve,” in which firms must first invest in organizational changes before realizing financial gains. relevance_to_project: 2026 survey paper. Useful for framing and identifying gaps our study fills.

citation: Acemoglu, Lelarge & Restrepo (2020), Competing with Robots: Firm-Level Evidence from France
authors: Acemoglu D, Lelarge C, Restrepo P
research_question: French firm-level: robot adoption → employment, labor share, productivity
main_findings: We study the firm-level implications of robot adoption in France. Of 55,390 firms in our sample, 598 adopted robots between 2010 and 2015, but these firms accounted for 20 percent of manufacturing employment. Adopters experienced significant declines in labor shares, the share of production workers in employment, and increases in value added and productivity. They expand their overall employment as well. However, this expansion comes at the expense of competitors, leading to an overall negative association between adoption and employment. Robot adoption has a large impact on the labor share because adopters are larger and grow faster than their competitors.
relevance_to_project: Key empirical evidence for displacement effect of automation at micro level. Supports the task model predictions.

citation: Acemoglu & Restrepo (2018), Low-Skill and High-Skill Automation
research_question: Theoretical: heterogeneous effects of automation by skill level
main_findings: We present a task-based model in which high- and low-skill workers compete against machines in the production of tasks. Low-skill (high-skill) automation corresponds to tasks performed by low-skill (high-skill) labor being taken over by capital. Automation displaces the type of labor it directly affects, depressing its wage. Through ripple effects, automation also affects the real wage of other workers. Counteracting these forces, automation creates a positive productivity effect, pushing up the price of all factors. Because capital adjusts to keep the interest rate constant, the productivity effect dominates in the long run. Finally, low-skill (high-skill) automation increases (reduces) wage inequality.
relevance_to_project: Important for our channel decomposition: automation effects differ by task/skill type.
